title: Rapid City man pleads guilty for 2024 Skyline Drive shooting
description: The second person involved in the volley of bullets fired from Skyline Drive that hit Kelly's Sports Lounge pleaded guilty on Tuesday, March 24.
_full_text:
The second person involved in the volley of bullets fired from Skyline Drive that hit Kelly's Sports Lounge pleaded guilty on Tuesday, March 24. 

Roman Venegas, 19, appeared for his change of plea hearing before Circuit Court Judge Jeffery Connolly at the Pennington County Courthouse.

Venegas was indicted for one count of aiding and abetting the discharge of a firearm at a structure and two counts of aiding and abetting aggravated assault. Per a plea agreement with the state, he pleaded guilty to the first count. The state agreed to dismiss the other charges. 

Around 9:20 p.m. on Friday, Dec. 27, officers were dispatched to a business in the 800 block of Jackson Boulevard in Rapid City on a report of a shooting. An investigation determined the gunfire originated from the 1300 block of Skyline Drive. Officers found several spent shell casings on Skyline Drive and rounds struck Kelly's in the 800 block of Jackson Boulevard. 

Assistant Police Chief Scott Sitts said after the shooting, video surveillance from the area revealed three individuals exiting a maroon SUV and taking turns shooting a pistol in the area. Sitts said the suspects later fled the area in the same vehicle. 

Venegas, his brother David Venegas, 24, and a juvenile male were arrested for their connection to the incident on Tuesday, Dec. 31, 2024. David was sentenced on Tuesday, March 17, to 15 years in prison with five suspended for discharging a firearm at a structure. The state dismissed all other charges. 

Roman Venegas' sentencing is scheduled for April 28 at 8:30 a.m. He faces up to 15 years in prison and up to a $30,000 fine.

title: Abortion pills are gaining ground as a method for ending pregnancies, and opponents are responding
description: As states that already ban abortion look to further restrict access this year, much of the focus is on pills sent by out-of-state providers.
_full_text:
As states that already ban abortion look to further restrict access this year, much of the focus is on pills sent by out-of-state providers.

A survey released Tuesday helps explain the emphasis. It suggests that more women in states with bans obtained abortions last year using the pills prescribed via telehealth than by traveling to places where it's legal.

Most of the states with the political will to impose broad bans have already done so in the nearly four years since the U.S. Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade and opened the door to enforcing them. So far this year, just one state has a new one.

Here’s a look at where things stand as many state legislatures are wrapping up or have completed their 2026 sessions.

States are taking steps to make abortion pills harder to get

South Dakota Gov. Larry Rhoden, a Republican, signed a bill last week that makes it a felony to advertise, distribute or sell abortion pills.

Similar measures have cleared both legislative chambers this year in Mississippi, where the House and Senate need to iron out differences before sending a bill to Republican Gov. Tate Reeves.

A survey of state abortion policies from the Guttmacher Institute, which supports abortion rights, finds that at least three states — Florida, Oklahoma and Texas — already have laws that specifically ban providers from mailing the pills to patients. Louisiana has classified mifepristone as a controlled dangerous substance.

Bills intended to keep out the pills have cleared one chamber in Arizona, Indiana and South Carolina this year. Republicans control the legislatures in all three states and the governor’s office in two of them. In Arizona, any restrictions that pass could be vetoed by Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs.

Survey suggests more women using abortion pills in states with bans

A Guttmacher survey released Tuesday sheds light on why abortion opponents may be focusing on pills.

The report suggests that in 2025, for the first time, more women in the 13 states that ban abortion at all stages of pregnancy obtained pills through telehealth than traveled to other states for abortion.

The prescriptions come from providers in states with laws adopted since the fall of Roe that are intended to protect those who prescribe abortion pills to patients in states with bans. Most often, women using pills for abortion are prescribed a regimen of two drugs — mifepristone and misoprostol. They're approved for use in the first 10 weeks of pregnancy.

The estimated increase in the mailing pills comes as Guttmacher’s estimates also suggest fewer women are traveling to obtain abortions in states like Colorado, Illinois, Kansas and New Mexico.

Guttmacher's estimates are based on data from a monthly survey conducted among a random sample of U.S. abortion providers, combined with historical data from every provider in the U.S. They reflect a trend documented in other surveys of abortion providers.

Court battles are also centered on pills

Multiple states are challenging the federal rules that allow mifepristone to be prescribed via telehealth. Requiring in-person prescriptions instead would at least dent the ability of out-of-state providers to get pills into states with bans.

Louisiana has such a lawsuit in federal court there; the attorneys general of Florida and Texas have one in Texas; those two states, along with Idaho, Kansas and Missouri, are making the same case in a Missouri court.

Meanwhile, Texas has filed civil cases and Louisiana criminal ones against providers accused of sending pills into their states.

The Food and Drug Administration last year approved a generic version of mifepristone, which frustrated abortion opponents.

One state imposed a ban, but its fate is uncertain

Wyoming is the only state this year that has imposed a new abortion ban.

Under a law signed in March by Republican Gov. Mark Gordon, it became the fifth state with a ban on abortion at about six weeks’ gestational age — before many women realize they’re pregnant. Like most of the others, Wyoming’s bans abortions once cardiac activity can be detected.

But courts have rejected previous Wyoming efforts to limit abortion, and the Wyoming Supreme Court in January struck down a ban on abortion at all stages of pregnancy.

The idea of punishing women is not gaining ground

No state has adopted a measure intended to allow criminal prosecutions against women who have abortions.

Proposals to do so keep getting made but sputter early in the legislative process.

The farthest such a bill has advanced was a hearing last year before a Senate subcommittee in South Carolina. One was scheduled for a subcommittee hearing in Tennessee this month, but didn’t get one.

Pregnancy Justice, which advocates for the rights of pregnant people, says it’s tracked new “abortion-as-homicide” measures introduced in six states in 2026 — down from 13 states last year.

The major established anti-abortion groups oppose the approach. “Women require compassion and support,” said Ingrid Duran, the state legislative director for National Right to Life. “Not prosecution.”

Melissa Murray, a professor at New York University School of Law, says that by introducing bills with penalties against women, the movement’s less compromising abolitionists can break down the idea that such policies are off-limits.

“You keep pushing the boundary, pushing the envelope, eventually you will get what you’re seeking,” Murray said. “It will no longer feel fanciful or shocking.”

She also noted that women are already sometimes charged with crimes related to their pregnancies. This month, police in Georgia charged a woman with murder after allegedly using an abortion pill and the opioid painkiller oxycodone.

Abortion will be on ballots in November

Abortion questions will be before voters in at least three states in November.

Missouri lawmakers are asking voters to repeal the right to reproductive freedom that they put into the state constitution in 2024.

Elsewhere, voters are being asked to add constitutional amendments that largely mirror current state abortion laws.

In Nevada, a state constitutional amendment to allow abortion until fetal viability — generally considered to be sometime after 21 weeks of pregnancy — passed in 2024, and needs voter approval a second time to take effect.

A Virginia ballot measure would guarantee the right to reproductive freedom, including access to contraception and making decisions on abortion care during the first two trimesters of pregnancy.

Associated Press reporter Amelia Thomson DeVeaux contributed to this article.

title: Darline Tinnell

July 27, 1931 - March 17, 2026

Darline Tinnell was born July 27, 1931, in Millboro, South Dakota, daughter of William and Addie (Rutherford) Janice. She attended kindergarten through high school in Colome, South Dakota, where she graduated in 1949.

Darline loved her family dearly, and always enjoyed parties, picnics and family reunions with them.

title: Rapid City man pleads guilty to rape charge in connection to assault at jail
description: A Rapid City man pleaded guilty Monday, March 23, to third-degree rape of a 53-year-old.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man pleaded guilty Monday, March 23, to third-degree rape of a 53-year-old. 

Trinity Twiss, 31, appeared for his change of plea hearing before Circuit Court Judge Joshua Hendrickson at the Pennington County Courthouse. 

Per an agreement with the state, Twiss pleaded guilty to third-degree rape and the state dismissed a number of misdemeanor charges. Both sides are open to comment at sentencing. 

According to court documents, Twiss has four pending charges, three of which are petty theft charges and the last for entering or refusing to leave property after notice.

The state alleged that on Oct. 29, 2025, Twiss committed third-degree rape of a 53-year-old person inside the Pennington County Jail while they were incapable to give consent. 

In South Dakota, third-degree rape is a Class 2 felony with a maximum sentence of 50 years in prison and $100,000 fine. 

Twiss' sentencing is set for 1 p.m. on May 18.

title: Wildfires update: Operating change for Qury, Coyote Flats fires
description: Officials said over the weekend fire crews are adjusting work on the Qury wildfire near Custer. Near Rockerville, the Coyote Flats fire is now 90% contained.
_full_text:
Officials said over the weekend fire crews are adjusting work on the Qury wildfire near Custer. Near Rockerville, the Coyote Flats fire is now 90% contained.

The interagency team leading the Qury fire effort said on Monday, March 23, command shifted to a local unit.

"Crews will continue to mop up and patrol control lines during the day," the team said. Sunday was the last night of overnight work.

"There will still be firefighters on the ground around the perimeter of the fire to reach 100% containment," officials said. The fire is 70% contained near Custer, according to Watch Duty.

"Although the organization is getting smaller, there will still be resources on the ground around the perimeter of the fire, continuing to mop up and patrol firelines," the team said in a release.

"The Type 3 Interagency Incident Management Team would like to thank the community of Custer for their support of firefighters throughout our assignment. We would also like to thank all of the local restaurants and hotels who provided food and accommodations," the team said. "The Volunteer Fire Departments, Custer Search and Rescue, Custer County Emergency Management, and Custer County Sheriff’s Office for their continued support with resources throughout the incident. The community has come together with interagency support to make this a successful operation."

Road closures: Lower French Creek Road from Highway 16A to Highway 87, Hazelrodt, Glen Erin, Flynn Creek, Herbert Draw, Highway 87 from 16A to the north boundary of Wind Cave National Park (the park remains open). Visit Custer County Sheriff's Facebook page for the most up-to-date information on road closures. https://www.facebook.com/custercountysd

Residential reopenings: Repopulation efforts have happened across the entire fire. Access is for residential traffic only. Residents must have the Orange Card issued by the Custer County Emergency Management.

Temporary Flight Restrictions: There is a Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) over the perimeter of the Qury Fire. Please do not fly any unauthorized aircraft over the fire. If you fly, we can’t.

Coyote Flats wildfire

Officials leading the Coyote Flats fire effort said Monday containment is at 90%.

 Blue heeler
Two women and a blue heeler inspect the fallout of the Coyote Flats wildfire on Thursday, March 19, outside Rockerville. 

"Firefighters will continue mop up around the fire perimeter and around structures until secure," officials said in a social media post. "Preventing wildland fires requires extreme caution with human-caused ignition sources. Please be very careful!"

The Sundance wildfire, near Argyle, affected 25 acres and is 100% contained, according to Watch Duty.

 Wildfire near Rockerville
The Coyote Flats wildfire had affected almost 400 acres as of Friday morning, March 19. No injuries have been reported and some outbuildings, but no homes, were destroyed.

title: 2 people arrested after traffic stop turned drug seizure in Rapid City
description: Two Rapid City people were arrested after a traffic stop escalated to a drug seizure on Friday, March 20.
_full_text:
Two Rapid City people were arrested after a traffic stop escalated to a drug seizure on Friday, March 20.

Gabriel Brown, 51, and Jennifer Wolfe, 37, were placed under arrest on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance. Brown has additional charges such as possession of drug paraphernalia, driving under suspension and no proof of insurance.

At around 9 p.m. on Friday, police pulled over a car with a license plate violation at around 300 block of East North Street.

Brown, the driver, and Wolfe, the passenger, were asked to step out of the vehicle as police conducted a K9 outside sniff of the vehicle. The K9 alerted the presence of illegal narcotics inside the car. 

A search of the car turned up psilocybin mushrooms, methamphetamine, associated paraphernalia and a loaded handgun magazine. 

Brown is being held at the Pennington County Jail on a $300 cash only bond. As of Monday, Wolfe was not found in the jail roster.

title: Housing jumpstarts revitalization of Herreid, SD, population 400
byline: By BART PFANKUCH/South Dakota News Watch - South Dakota News Watch
description: Dick Werner drives his pickup through the streets of this northern South Dakota town and can hardly go a block before stopping to point out new houses or apartments that have popped up in the past few years.
_full_text:
Dick Werner drives his pickup through the streets of this northern South Dakota town and can hardly go a block before stopping to point out new houses or apartments that have popped up in the past few years.

To be sure, the journey doesn’t last long because the agricultural outpost of Herreid – located 7 miles south of the North Dakota border – only has about 400 residents and just a few streets that splinter off U.S. Highway 83, the main drag through town.

And yet, Werner’s excitement is palpable as he shares the stories of how he and other local leaders have spurred a rebirth of the ranching and outdoor recreation town’s population and economy by adding new housing, new municipal amenities and new businesses.

Werner, 68, is a retired banking executive who served in the state Legislature and on the Beadle County Commission before taking on a volunteer role as president of Herreid Area Housing Development (HAHD.)

He grew up in Herried but spent adulthood elsewhere, returning to his hometown in 2016 to find the local grocery store shuttered and the school population at 109, just above the threshold where state law requires dissolution of the school and assimilation into a larger nearby district.

“If you lose your school and your grocery store, your town is in trouble,” Werner said.

Herreid’s population peaked in 1960 at 767 people and has been on the decline since, falling to 416 in 2020, according to the U.S. Census. School enrollment followed suit as the population grew older and fewer families with children made Herreid their home.

Since returning, Werner has played a key role in finding the money and providing the expertise to begin rebuilding the housing stock in Herreid, a critical first step in attracting families and workers who form the backbone of the future for any small town in South Dakota.

“We were really hurting for homes because without places to live, there’s no way people can move here to work or raise a family,” he said.

Response: Grants and local donors drive growth

As with many small, remote South Dakota communities, Herreid is not a target for privately funded housing projects, largely because profit margins for developers are too narrow and it is difficult for them to get materials and skilled workers.

Instead, Herreid has turned to a unique funding model to find money for new homes and apartments.

Over the past decade, Werner has obtained $2.2 million in grants to help pay for land, materials and construction for development of new housing, for rehabilitation of existing homes and businesses, and for municipal projects including a pool, playground and sports complex.

The list of funders is varied and includes among others the South Dakota Housing Authority, U.S. Department of Agriculture Rural Development, the Land, Water and Conservation Fund, Grow South Dakota, the South Dakota Community Foundation, Wellmark Foundation, WEB Water, CoBank, MDU Resources, Campbell County Bank, Federal Home Loan Banks, and Homes Are Possible Inc. in Aberdeen.

Meanwhile, the HAHD created another pool of about $180,000 in available funds by soliciting loans from 30 local residents who were committed to supporting future growth. The group also raised more than $250,000 from fundraising events, including $200,000 from a Queen of Diamonds raffle program held in a local tavern.

“You just have to know where to get the money,” Werner said. “You’ve got to have connections and partnerships, but it’s important to know that there are resources across the state to help your community.”

Evidence: New homes, apartments in Herreid

Successful projects have followed the funding.

HAHD has developed five speculative homes that were sold before completion and brought 25 new residents to town. It has helped new residents pay for single-family and twin homes through the state Governor’s House program, in which houses are built by prison inmates.

HAHD has acquired 16 apartment units that are fully occupied. Its next proposed project is construction of a new three-unit apartment building for which Werner just landed $700,000 in grants. The organization has also helped pay for improvements to 18 existing homes.

The HAHD is now trying to sell and support development of single-family homes on eight lots just north of downtown that are already served by sewer and water lines. The group is selling the full-size lots for only $7,000 and can provide assistance to buyers to keep their construction and mortgage costs low, Werner said.

“We want to get people into homes, to own them and have pride of ownership,” Werner said.

Insights: Economic growth follows housing

If housing is the platform that enables growth in a community, economic development is the resulting engine that can propel forward prosperity for individuals and families.

Through a team effort among town leaders, engaged local residents, the HAHD and the Herreid Economic Development Corp., the town has seen positive growth on several fronts in recent years.

Melinda Neeley, president of the development corporation, said expansion of housing in Herreid has set the stage for arrival of new businesses and residents that have stabilized the local economy.

Even with only 400 people, Herreid now boasts a thriving grocery store, medical clinic, pharmacy, bank, day care, livestock yard, diesel repair shop, and hardware and feed store.

Tax money, grants and local donations were used to help pay for a $200,000 sports complex, a $145,000 upgrade of a downtown playground, and a $1.1 million municipal pool upgrade as well a $20,000 addition of local pickleball courts.

The local K-12 public school just completed a $4 million addition and renovation project.

“I feel like we’re on an upward trajectory,” Neeley told News Watch. “It takes the help of people from all of these different groups to not just maintain what we have but to grow the services we have here.”

Moreover, the slow but steady rebuilding of the local economy has attracted or strengthened employers that are providing good jobs — including the Pig Improvement Co., Agtegra Cooperative, and a host of area ranchers and farmers as well as agricultural supply and trucking companies, Neeley said.

The volunteer economic development group owns several land lots in town that it has made available for development at low cost, Neeley said. The group also owns a few buildings downtown that it is able to rent at reasonable rates, lowering the entry and ongoing costs of businesses that want to make a go of it.

Additionally, the group has taken steps to create succession plans for business owners who plan to retire or leave town, Neeley said. The organization tries to provide affordable rent and other assistance to increase viability of new businesses or for businesses with new ownership.

“If they had to purchase the building, the cost would be much greater,” she said. “The loss of a single business could make a huge impact on our sales tax collection and the housing progress we’ve made.”

Limitations: Positive signs but work to do

Kayla and Preston Huber moved to Herreid seven years ago, and even though Kayla is a nurse, she jumped headlong into entrepreneurship by opening a grocery store called Fresh Start Market.

When she arrived, Herreid did not have a grocery store, and food options were limited within a drivable distance.

She and her husband downsized into a double-wide trailer house that they renovated because housing options were almost non-existent when they arrived.

“We got lucky when we moved back because there weren’t many options (for housing),” she said.

Huber said she has had great success and attributes some of that to efforts to generate new housing in town. “As a business owner, it’s definitely great to see,” she said.

And yet, she sees more opportunity for community growth if more housing can be developed.

“More people want to get out of cities and big-city life, and this is a great place to raise a family,” she said. “We’ve had some younger couples move back, and I know a few more that would move back if there were more places to live.”

Werner predicts that Herreid’s population will show a jump in the 2030 census, which would be the first increase in 70 years. Werner has also done calculations that he said show enrollment in the Herreid school will rise to about 150 in the next four years.

Some of the population growth has come from about 40 Hispanic residents who are in South Dakota on three-year federal work visas, some of whom have purchased homes in Herreid. The town has also attracted several Hutterite families who have moved to town in recent years, Werner said.

“If you’re in small, rural communities in South Dakota, and you’re not willing to accept diversity, you’re not going to grow because the Norwegians, Swedes, Germans and Polish are done coming here,” he said.

Werner said there’s no “special sauce” to generating growth in rural areas but that it does require an individual or a group to step up and get things started. But he cautions that it takes perseverance to keep the momentum and an ability to ignore the doubters.

“Housing development is a marathon, not a sprint, and you’ve got to have people who are committed,” he said. “There’s been all these naysayers out there on all these projects, but once they see the results, the naysayers all go away.”

During the drive around Herreid in February, Werner said that all the time and effort he has put into promoting housing and economic growth in his hometown have been well worth it.

“Probably the toughest thing you can do is to develop a property,” he said. “I’ve run banks, groups of banks and managed dozens of employees, and this is the toughest thing I’ve ever done. But it’s also the most rewarding.”

This story was originally published by South Dakota News Watch and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

title: Rapid City police catch 'repeat' wrong-way driver twice in one night, leading to DUI charges
description: A Box Elder man was arrested for DUI after a crash early Sunday, March 22, after he being stopped by police earlier in the night.
_full_text:
A Box Elder man was arrested for DUI after a crash early Sunday, March 22, after he being stopped by police earlier in the night.

Michael Reyna, 23, was arrested on suspicion of a driving under the influence, second-degree eluding, leaving the scene of a crash and wrong-way driving. 

Saturday night, police saw a black Dodge pickup driving the wrong way down Saint Joseph Street in downtown Rapid City. Police pulled the truck over and identified Reyna as the driver. 

Reyna was found not impaired and was given a warning about the traffic offense before being sent on his way.

At around 2 a.m. on Sunday, March 22, the same officer witnessed the same pickup driving the wrong way down Saint Joseph Street before Reyna turned the correct direction.

Police said Reyna didn't pull over when the officer attempted to stop the pickup and a pursuit ensued. During the chase, Reyna headed north on West Boulevard and struck a center median, which caused significant damage to the pickup.

Reyna continued north on I-190 before the truck eventually came to a stop due to its damage. Reyna was ordered out of the vehicle, where police noticed the smell of alcohol coming from him.

Reyna is being held at the Pennington County Jail with no bond.

title: Summer Nights set to return to Rapid City after Salamun's tiebreaking vote
description: After much discourse from local business owners, Rapid City Council members were at odds over a special-event alcohol license needed for the return of Summer Nights, a free concert series in downtown Rapid City. With council members split 4-4, Mayor Jason Salamun cast a tie-breaking "yes" vote during the council's Monday, March 16 meeting.
_full_text:
After much discourse from local business owners, Rapid City Council members were at odds over a special-event alcohol license needed for the return of Summer Nights, a free concert series in downtown Rapid City. With council members split 4-4, Mayor Jason Salamun cast a tie-breaking "yes" vote during the council's Monday, March 16 meeting.

The vote marks a solidification of a Summer Nights comeback after a 2025 hiatus.

 Summer Nights
Business owners in downtown Rapid City voiced concerns over Summer Nights returning to the area. 

Bill Evans (Ward 2), Lindsay Seachris (Ward 2), Greg Strommen (Ward 3) and John Roberts (Ward 4) voted no on the alcohol license. The yes vote group was Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) Josh Biberdorf (Ward 1), Kevin Maher (ward 3) and Callie Meyer (Ward 5). Lance Lehmann (Ward 4) and Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5) were absent.

The license is a malt-liquor license for the dates of May 28, June 4, 11, 18 and 25; July 2, 9, 16, 23 and 30; and August 6, 13, 20 and 27.

The event was hosted in the heart of downtown Rapid City every Thursday night from 5 p.m. to 9 p.m. from Memorial Day to Labor Day for 17 years until last year. In early 2025, the Summer Nights board announced the series would pause, citing the Rapid City Council's denial of their permit for their preferred location and an inability to find a new location during negotiations with city staff.  

In January 2026, Summer Nights secured a special event permit to hold the event at a new location — on Sixth Street, with the stage on Kansas City Street. Summer Nights previously suggested the location in 2025, but city staff turned it down because of the active construction of Block 5, which is now complete.

 Sandy Schwan
Summer Nights President Sandy Schwan addresses public concerns and city officials' questions about the return of Summer Nights, with a relocation to Sixth Street. 

The event core was previously at the intersection of Saint Joseph Street and Seventh Street, with the stage on Seventh Street.

Leading up to Monday's contentious alcohol license vote, business owners near the new concert series area voiced their concerns to the council. 

Former state Sen. Julie Frye-Mueller, who owns a building at 626 Sixth Street — which used to house Roger Frye's Paint and Supply store — said the interference would disturb her tenants and suggested the event move out of the downtown area.

 Julie Frye-Mueller
Julie Frye-Muller requested the council consider moving the event to another part of Rapid City, outside of the downtown area. 

"I would really like you to take this off, off the agenda. I mean, it's already on the agenda, but I'd like it to be put off, if we can, so that we can have some discussions about it. We just found out about this last week, so we've been in the dark," Mueller said.

Her husband, Mike Mueller, followed up by reading a letter of concern from one of their tenants, Free Your Mind Massage owner Breanna Mendenhall.

"Based on current scheduling and client patterns, I estimate this change could cost my business at minimum $1,000 or more per month in lost income. It would also likely cost me 20 hours of lost appointment slots due to this proposal. There's also concern about the risk of property damage, vandalism or excessive trash behind the large events," Mueller read.

 Karl Koth
Hay Camp Brewery owner Karl Koth requested the council delay action to give further time for business owners and Summer Nights officials to address concerns.  

Hay Camp Brewery co-owner Karl Koth said, "All I'm asking here again is just bringing the impacted businesses and stakeholders to the table before we issue the ... permit and let's just remove that item."

Cohort Craft Brewery owner Jason Kingbury said, "Small businesses will be affected by this. Hay Camp will be in direct competition with Summer Nights as an event and as a beer venue. I urge the council to postpone the special permit vote tonight until more collaboration can occur."

In 2025, council members cited safety concerns regarding the closure of St. Joseph Street for the event, and several businesses expressed frustrations with Summer Nights reportedly affecting business by deterring locals from the downtown area and consolidating commerce to food trucks and vendors at Summer Nights.

"Anybody else downtown that has questions or concerns that wants to talk to us, we're also aware that, because this is a new location, and some of the footprint lays out differently than our previous footprint, we might have to make some adjustments," Summer Nights President Sandy Schwan said at the meeting.

Council members then discussed why they would be voting for or against the permit.

 Summer Nights map
A map of Summer Nights events on Sixth Street with the general setup for the summer. 

Council members Evans, Seachris, Strommen and Roberts voiced their concerns and reasons for voting "no," citing the business owners' concerns as presented in pubic discussion. Strommenn requested that the license vote be moved to the next council meeting.

The council voted 4-4 on the matter.

Salamun, as the tiebreaker, requested further input from Schwan before making his decision. He asked Schwan how Summer Nights would be affected if the council delayed action.

Schwan said, "It's going to cripple us. ... That's a decision I would have to take back to the board, they put a great deal of time planning this and as most of you know, it's an all-volunteer board."

She cited that at this point in their timeline, a new location couldn't be considered and would essentially end Summer Nights altogether.

Salamun voted "no" on delaying action to the next council meeting in April.

 Summer Nights
A scene from Summer Nights in 2024. After the concert series was canceled in 2025, it's set to return this year. 

He then voted "yes" in another 4-4 vote, on approval of the special event alcohol license, with the stipulation Summer Nights speak further with the local businesses to try to please all parties.

Salamun clarified to the Journal after the meeting, "It's tough to make everybody happy, because I think some requests are reasonable, some are not. So my tiebreak vote tonight was really dependent upon the expectation that Summer Nights does their best to provide reasonable accommodations to the impacted businesses within the footprint."

"If this is about Summer Nights actually happening or not, I still want it to happen, but my expectation is that they still work with the downtown business owners," he said. "They still have several months to do that within that footprint."

title: 'We're all in this together': Black Hills businesses rally together to support firefighters
byline: Madison Willis and Tanya Manus Journal staff
description: The rhythmic thud of Louis Umbarger’s knife hitting the cutting board sounded like a frantic drum solo, keeping time with the organized chaos of the Custer Beacon kitchen.
_full_text:
The rhythmic thud of Louis Umbarger’s knife hitting the cutting board sounded like a frantic drum solo, keeping time with the organized chaos of the Custer Beacon kitchen.

As the owner worked through a mountain of ham, his most trusted employees, Yvette Morrow, Leroy Pierce, Daria Kovalova and Jaxon Phillips, blurred past him as they helped assemble and cook 400 breakfast burritos before the sun could go down on the Black Hills on Thursday, March 19.

 Cutting meat
Yvette Morrow preps meat at Custer Beacon before dinner rush. The restaurant had a limited menu on Thursday, March 19, so they could dedicate time and supplies to feeding firefighters.  

For a week, the Qury Fire has been a hungry beast, devouring more than 9,000 acres of timber and brush. Umbarger, along with the entire community of Custer and beyond, is determined that the men and women fighting the fire don't go hungry themselves.

“I think it’s in Custer’s bones that we’re all in this together,” Umbarger said. “Ever since we’ve started there has been this mentality of the (rising) tide raises all ships. If one of us does good, we’re all doing good. This is just another example of everyone in real-time figuring out how to rise and meet the moment.”

It's been a week since the Qury wildfire started outside Custer on a warm and windy day. Since then, the fire's footprint has increased to 9,163 acres, over 14 square miles. Containment is at 57%. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.

But, at a press briefing on Thursday, March 19, Todd Hoover, the fire's incident commander, said, "We're feeling fairly confident now that we have the final acreage of the fire." The fire only grew 2 acres between Thursday and Friday, with containment rising from 44% to 57%.

 Paper bag Part 1
Rapid Valley School District donated food to emergency personnel responding to the Qury wildfire. Each food bag was decorated by a student showing their support.   

As firefighters have been working day in and day out to contain the fire, businesses from Custer, Hill City and Rapid City have been preparing to do their part to support in anyway they can.

From thank you messages from school districts to food donations from residents, it has been an all-hands-on-deck experience for the Black Hills. 

For Umbarger, he knew something foreboding was coming when he saw smoke coming from the skyline last week. He said there were a few hours where Custer was in this smoky haze, before the winds shifted and pushed the fire in the opposite direction. 

 Thank You
Residents from Custer, Rapid City and Hill City and elsewhere have been donating food and drinks at Custer School District. 

"I drove up to the hillside over there and you could see the orange flames just running up the hill," Umbarger said. "So that was definitely like an immediate anxiety factor of like what do you do?"

Umbarger  and several restaurants were called the first night of the fire, Thursday, March 12. Unfortunately, Umbarger had to decline the first night due to lack of resources and staff, however, he said he still wanted to help and asked to be given another opportunity when he could restock. 

"A lot of different kitchen owners that I know were jumping in to help, burning their own time to make sure they had what they needed," Umbarger said. 

Umbarger and his team got another chance to aid the effort on Monday, March 16, by making 400 lunches for the firefighters. He said the restaurant is prepped for catering and handling large events, so tackling 400 orders wasn't really a concern when given enough prep time. However, smaller restaurants weren't able to fulfill that exact amount, but still made smaller orders ranging from 100 to 200.  

 Seasoning the meat
Leroy Pierce seasons meat used for Thursday's dinner for firefighters. 

Umbarger said this effort became community-wide, where business and restaurant owners banded together to ensure everyone had the proper resources, staff and space to prepare meals for the firefighters. 

"It's a big deal and it felt meaningful to be of service in the little ways we can," Umbarger said. 

 Custer Beacon
Leroy Pierce, Yvette Morrow, Daria Kovalova, Louis Umbarger, and Jaxon Philips work diligently to ensure every firefighter working on the Qury wildfire receives a freshly cooked meal.  

The restaurant was called upon again on Thursday, March 19, to prepare breakfast for the firefighters. The Custer Beacon is known as being the "good vibes factory" and Umbarger said, with each burrito made, he hoped he could show that to the men and women who are risking their lives. 

Umbarger wanted to leave one final message and that is, "Custer is still kicking." 

"Custer is durable. The grass is going to grow in really thick this spring as a result of this fire. I'm sure of it," Umbarger said. 

Black Hills Bagels

When South Dakota Wildland Fire asked for breakfast for 400 firefighters, Black Hills Bagels in Rapid City didn’t hesitate to help. With about 12 hours’ notice, the locally owned business assembled breakfast sandwiches, plus fruit and cookies, to feed the hard-working fire crew.

SDWF, which is part of the South Dakota Department of Public Safety, contacted Black Hills Bagels owner Eric Hawronski on the afternoon of Tuesday, March 17.

 Paper Bags Part 2
Rapid Valley School District donated food to all of the emergency personnel responding to the Qury wildfire, with personalized bags. 

“I was very thankful I have an awesome crew,” a weary but happy Hawronski said on Thursday, March 19. “As soon as I found out (we were making breakfast), my mixing department was already mixing dough and I said, ‘So, we need an extra 40 dozen bagels.’ They stayed late and were able to make the dough so that we were able to do it."

Hawronski and five of his staff were back at Black Hills Bagels early on Wednesday, March 18, to assemble the firefighters’ breakfasts.

 Cutting ham
Louis Umbarger dices ham that will be used in breakfast burritos to feed firefighters at the Qury wildfire. 

“We did make 400 sandwiches in the middle of the night so they could have breakfast,” Hawronski said. “We had to have a team come in at 2 in the morning to get them done. It was four hours of crazy, crazy running. ... A bunch of people came in on their day off. ... It was a lot of mouths to feed.”

Year-round, Black Hills Bagels offers discounts for first responders, Hawronski said. He’s grateful his business had the chance to do more to help amid the ongoing fires.

Millstone Family Restaurants

For the Millstone Family Restaurants in Rapid City, having two locations is a bonus when they’re called on to feed firefighters.

Greta Holzwarth co-owns the Millstone restaurants with her parents, Jerry and Joan Scriver. Millstone has had federal or local contracts to feed firefighters for more than 20 years, she said.

 Donations of food
Residents from Custer, Rapid City and Hill City and elsewhere have donated food and drinks for firefighters. 

On Thursday, March 19, for example, the westside Millstone provided 60 dinners to go — hamburgers, salad and cake — for the crew battling the Coyote Flats fire.  

Other times, Millstone’s staff has pulled overnight shifts to make breakfast for as many as 300, Holzwarth said.

 Sliced meat
Yvette Morrow preps meat at Custer Beacon before dinner rush on Thursday, March 19. 

“One of the more wild incidents recently was when we had the horrible, horrible windstorms and there was that fire burning by Custer. They had called us the night before. They called us right before closing and (firefighters) were going to need 300 breakfasts and 300 sack lunches, and unfortunately we lost power that morning,” Holzwarth said.

 BRIGHT SIDE LOGO.jpg

Without electricity, the westside Millstone was able to provide the sack lunches. Fortunately, the Millstone on North Lacrosse Street still had electricity, and staff there was able to help prepare firefighters' meals, Holzwarth said.

Depending on fire crews’ needs, requests for meals can come with as little as an hour’s notice.

“Between the two (locations), we’re able to cover quite a bit of ground on short notice,” Holzwarth said. “We’re geared for it. We’re used to it. Once we hear (about) fires, we kind of think (meal requests) will come in. We do know it's very short notice. Our staff is fantastic.”

Other businesses that assisted in the food effort are: 

HJEM A.M. - Custer
Marias - Custer
Miner's Cup - Custer
Sage Creek Grill - Custer
Buglin' Bull Restaurant and Sports Bar - Custer
Custer State Game Lodge - Custer
Hill City Cake - Hill City 
Our Place - Custer
Hitch Rail Bar and Restaurant - Pringle 
Subway - Custer
The Custer Wolf - Custer
Frontier Bar and Grill - Custer
Lynn's Dakotamart - Custer

title: South Dakota DCI investigating after troopers fire at suspect in Box Elder
description: The South Dakota Attorney's General's office said the Division of Criminal Investigation, at the request of the South Dakota Highway Patrol, is investigating an officer-involved shooting occurred Saturday, March 21, in Box Elder.
_full_text:
The South Dakota Attorney's General's office said the Division of Criminal Investigation, at the request of the South Dakota Highway Patrol, is investigating an officer-involved shooting occurred Saturday, March 21, in Box Elder.

The incident started when the Meade County Sheriff’s Office initiated a pursuit with a vehicle, the AG's office said. The Highway Patrol located the vehicle in Box Elder. After a short pursuit, the vehicle was stopped. At one point, troopers fired at the suspect, who suffered non‑life‑threatening injuries, according to the AG's office.

No one else was injured. The suspect’s name will be released at a later time, the AG's office said.

The AG's office said additional details would be part of a final report and did not answer specific questions about whether the suspect fired shots or how many shots troopers fired.

“The Attorney General’s Office and DCI approach every officer‑involved shooting with the utmost seriousness, and DCI will carry out a complete and unbiased investigation into this incident,” Jackley said in the release. “We will release our findings once our work is finished. There is no continuing danger to the public.”

In its investigation, DCI will process the crime scene, conduct a forensic examination of all collected evidence, interview those involved, and review all available video. DCI will issue a summary once the investigation is completed. The Box Elder Police Department, the Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office are assisting DCI.

The Highway Patrol is cooperating in the investigation, the office said. This is the shooting involving an officer in South Dakota this year.

title: Pennington County Commission briefed on new property tax laws
description: In the aftermath of the 2026 South Dakota Legislature session, Pennington County Director of Equalization Shannon Rittberger gave county commissioners an update on the approved 2026 South Dakota Legislature bills that impact property taxes.
_full_text:
In the aftermath of the 2026 South Dakota Legislature session, Pennington County Director of Equalization Shannon Rittberger gave county commissioners an update on the approved 2026 South Dakota Legislature bills that impact property taxes.

Both shift the tax burden away from property tax and onto sales taxes. 

Senate Bill 96 provides counties the ability to increase sales tax by up to 0.5% (including on cities within the counties), with the proceeds dedicated to reducing property taxes on owner-occupied property. The other property tax bill Rittberger discussed, SB 245, is very similar to SB 96, with the exception that it takes place at the state level, he noted.

It's unclear if the county commission will utilize SB 96. The commissioners did not vote on any new tax laws at the meeting.

Senate Bill 96

Rittberger said the earliest a county could collect sales tax would be January of 2027. 

"The proceeds of that (potential new sales tax) will have to go directly to offset owner-occupied property taxes in the county ... and offset owner-occupied property taxes by a percentage, so property that has a higher property tax will have a higher dollar savings than a property with a lower property tax," Rittberger told the Pennington County Commission on Tuesday, March 18.

According to previous reporting by the Journal, the current sales tax rate in Rapid City is 6.2%.

 Property tax
Pennington County Director of Equalization Shannon Rittberger presented at the Tuesday, March 17, Pennington County Board of Commissioners about a pair of Senate bills regarding property tax relief efforts.  

The Governor's Office released an interactive map showcasing the savings from the bill. As an example of the property tax relief provided by the bill, Rettiberger said that if a home in Pennington County were assessed at $325,000, its property tax would decrease by 22%.

The 2026 levy rate for Pennington County is 2.488 and a home valued at $325,000 would have a property tax of $808.60. The estimated 22% decrease in property tax brought on by SB 96 would then decrease the cost to roughly $603.70.

A mill levy is a property tax based on the assessed value of a property. One mill is equal to one dollar per $1,000 of assessed value. The levy is calculated by determining the amount of revenue each jurisdiction needs to fund its budget for public services, such as schools, parks and libraries.

"I've got to say, this is all a guess. We don't know if Pennington County, or any other county, is going to institute a sales tax and how much, and then we don't know how much sales tax would be collected by that if it's done. And we don't know how much owner-occupied property taxes will be and when this could actually happen," Rittberger said. "So this is a guess. It's not perfect, but it gives us an idea."

Commissioner Ron Rossknecht (District 1) asked what counties would be most impacted by such an option, since there are vast county population differences in South Dakota.

Rittberger, using the interactive map, noted for a property valued at $325,000 in Harding County the property tax savings would be up to 20%.

"I asked that question to the Department of Revenue. You got to assume that Harding County doesn't have the sales tax revenue that Pennington County does, but Harding County also doesn't have as many owner-occupied properties paying a property tax. So what little is collected goes a long way to offset," Rittberger replied.

He also said changes to the sales tax rate, if made by the county, could not be done before the law takes effect, which is on July 1, 2026. He also said he's unsure if these relief efforts will be felt by residents until 2028 or 2029.

Rittberger said he thought it was a good idea that the funding collected from sales tax would go to a designated fund that cannot be used for anything other than property taxes.

Senate Bill 245

According to reporting by South Dakota Searchlight, Senate Bill 245 would capture $114 million in ongoing annual revenue from next year’s scheduled increase of the statewide sales tax rate from 4.2% to 4.5%.

This funding would then be used to reduce local school district property taxes on owner-occupied homes.

Additionally, $56 million would be taken from state reserves to get the effort started before the sales tax rate increase.

Rittberger said this school levy offset will contribute to lowering property taxes. He also added the county doesn't have anything to do with this effort.

 Property tax
The interactive map provided by the governor's office gives an estimated value of property tax relief implement by SB 96 and SB 245. 

Commissioner Jerry Derr (District 4) said, "Senate Bill 245, so in essence, the state, under the school aid formula, is going to fill in more of that bucket and lessen that burden on owner-occupied properties."

Rittbereg agreed with his statement.

In the same interactive map, a home in Pennington County valued at $325,000 would pay $547 in property taxes that would go to the school, saving an estimated 13%.

Rittberg told the Journal after the meeting this property tax relief might affect property owners sooner due the state providing the immediate $56 million into the funding.

Commissioner Gary Drewes (District 5) said, "I would think that adding in the sales tax that's paid by many people, other than just South Dakota residents, that this is going to be a win for taxpayers."

Rossknecht and Derr noted that this may be targeted as property tax relief that equalizes across the board from sales tax revenue.

Derr said it's essentially still just another tax.

No action was taken at the meeting, as this was a presentation. Absent at the meeting were commissioners Deb Hadcock (District 3) and Ron Weifenbach (District 2).

title: Crews have Sundance wildfire in southern Custer County 50% contained
description: Another wildfire in Custer County, near Argyle, is 50% contained, according to officials.
_full_text:
Another wildfire in Custer County, near Argyle, is 50% contained, according to officials.

The Custer County Sheriff's Office said around 6:15 p.m. on Saturday, March 21, that half of the Sundance wildfire is contained. The fire affected about 25 acres as of Saturday evening, according to Watch Duty.

according to the sheriff's office. Several fire agencies collaborated, utilizing two helicopters and an aircraft "to tackle the flames."

 032226-nws-sundance-p1.jpg
Crews were called to the scene of the Sundance wildfire around 3:25 p.m. on Friday, March 20. 

Because the fire was near residences, officials issued an evacuation order. "Firefighters worked tirelessly to contain the fire, successfully stopping its forward movement," the sheriff's office said.

The South Dakota Department of Public Safety said in a Saturday morning update fire activity had slowed after strong strategic efforts on Friday. Federal, state and local resources continue working to secure fire lines. They are tracking down and extinguishing hot spots on the fire’s edge, DPS said.

"The goal of (Friday) night's air tanker drop of fire retardant was to help prevent any movement to the east," DPS said. "A burnout operation (Friday) evening proactively eliminated available fire fuels in the area between the outer dozer fire line and the interior fire, thereby creating a strong fuel break along the fire’s east edge."

According to Custer County Emergency Management, Lone Elk Drive and all connecting roads, as well as Mountain Lion Lane and all of its connecting roads, were affected. Traffic was set to be open to local residents at 7 p.m. Saturday.

Argyle is about 61 miles southwest of Rapid City.

The sheriff's office thanked Custer County Communications Center, Search and Rescue, Emergency Management, Hot Springs and Custer Ambulance Services, Fall River County Sheriff’s Office, and the South Dakota Highway Patrol for their crucial assistance.

title: Authorities, organizations look for missing man last seen in Wounded Knee area
description: A number of organizations have put out word about a missing man last seen in the Wounded Knee area.
_full_text:
A number of organizations have put out word about a missing man last seen in the Wounded Knee area.

The Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said Dale Warrior Sr., 67, was last seen around Feb. 27, around Wounded Knee. He's described as a Native American man, 5-foot-9, 225 pounds, with a medium build and brown complexion. He has black hair and brown eyes.

Okichile, a grassroots Missing and Murdered Indigenous People organization, is among those assisting the search. In a March 20 update, a Facebook page dedicated to the search said the account would take anonymous tips.

 032226-nws-warrior-p1.JPG
The Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said Dale Warrior Sr., 67, was last seen around Feb. 27, in the Wounded Knee area. 

"I Believe together as OYATE (family) of SOUTH DAKOTA TOGETHER We Can Bring Dale Home Now," the page said in a post.

The Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said Warrior was last seen driving a silver 2006 Pontiac Grand Prix GT2 with Nebraska plates 9C3862 (registration expired). He is a registered sex offender, the department said.

"Law enforcement has been actively following up on leads but has not yet been able to locate him," the department said in a Friday, March 20 update.

Anyone with information about Warrior's whereabouts is asked to contact Investigator Perez at 605-441-5141 or aromeroperez@ostdps.org, Case Agent Plume at 605-454-1790 or OSTDPS dispatch at 605-867-5111.

 032226-nws-warrior-p2.JPG
Police described Warrior as a Native American man, 5-foot-9, 225 pounds, with a medium build and brown complexion. He has black hair and brown eyes. 

"Any information, no matter how small, may help bring him home safely," tribal authorities said.

"Thanks to Francisca Okichile Tobacco , Federal Bureau Investigators, Special Agents, Criminal Investigators & Oglala Sioux Police Department (Correctional officers) for their Everyday Search as We Continue Work together We Appreciate you all," the Missing Indigenous Dale Warrior Sr. Facebook page said.

 032226-nws-warrior-p3.jpg
The Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said Warrior was last seen driving a silver 2006 Pontiac Grand Prix GT2 with Nebraska plates 9C3862.

title: Qury wildfire update: Containment at 68%, crews continue work
description: The Qury wildfire near Custer remains steady at 9,168 acres, with containment at 68%, according to the interagency team leading the effort.
_full_text:
The Qury wildfire near Custer remains steady at 9,168 acres, with containment at 68%, according to the interagency team leading the effort.

From a Saturday, March 21 update:

Current Situation: Demobilization of crews continues today as mop-up is implemented around the fire perimeter. Crews are becoming available for new incidents emerging throughout the Black Hills and around the region as containment of this fire increases. Rehabilitation of interior dozer and hand lines continues as crews work to restore the landscape to the way it was before the fire.

 pio_8x11_land_20260320_2156_Qury_SDSDS260142_0321day.jpg
An updated map of the Qury wildfire near Custer issued on Saturday, March 21. 

Warm and dry conditions are predicted throughout the day and into the evening. Dust will remain in the area on roadways with firefighting efforts. Residents are asked to please drive slowly, keep your head lights on, and be prepared for low visibility on gravel roads. Although smoke will be visible, firefighters will continue to be vigilant around the fire lines, and necessary staffing remains on the line.

Weather: Maximum temperature today will be around 82 degrees, wind speeds 10-15mph and relative humidity around 8%. A cold front is expected to move in this evening into Sunday morning. This will bring cooler temperatures, higher humidity and slightly stronger winds.

 Hotshot
A member of the Augusta Interagency Hotshots looks out onto the hills.

title: David Johnson running to return to South Dakota Legislature
description: David Johnson has announced his candidacy for re-election to the South Dakota Senate for Legislative District 33, which includes portions of Pennington County in the central Black Hills, north and south Rapid City and the communities of Black Hawk and Summerset in Meade County.
_full_text:
David Johnson has announced his candidacy for re-election to the South Dakota Senate for Legislative District 33, which includes portions of Pennington County in the central Black Hills, north and south Rapid City and the communities of Black Hawk and Summerset in Meade County.

Sen. Curt Voight currently holds the seat.

 DSC_7346RTKF - Copy.jpg
David Johnson 

“I’m proud of my long history serving the people of western South Dakota,” the Republican said in a release. “I’m a life-long resident of the Black Hills and the owner/operator of a local small business for 47 years. I’ve served this region as a state legislator and I’ve always felt it’s my civic duty to give back to the community that has supported my family and my business for so many years. My experience as a local businessman gives me particular insight into the issues confronting South Dakota's small business entrepreneurs and employee wage earners.”

Johnson was elected to the state House of Representatives in 2016 and served for four years. In 2020, he was elected to the state Senate served from 2021 through 2024. He served on the Appropriations, Veterans Affairs, and State Commerce committees, among others. Johnson served eight years as an officer in the US Air Force and Reserves, graduated from the South Dakota School of Mines in 1983 and the University of South Dakota with an MBA in 2003. He is the owner of a local tree nursery and tree care business and has been known locally as “The Tree Guy” for decades.

“My time in the state legislature was marked by the challenges of Covid and the national turmoil that followed. I was proud to be involved in keeping South Dakota's small businesses and general economic condition above the fray that was overwhelming many other parts of the country. South Dakota remained open for business, and the state legislature played a substantial role in that effort,” Johnson said in the release. “My primary objective in the Senate has been reducing the size of state government and the resulting costs, burdens, and taxes that over-regulation imposes on families and small service businesses.”

Johnson grew up in Rapid City. Following his graduation from the School of Mines, he was employed for a time as a software engineer for Honeywell Corp and Hughes Aircraft Companies. He returned to Rapid City in 1991 with his wife, Karen, and their three daughters. He has served as the president of Rapid City’s Right to Life organization and currently serves on the board for two statewide youth/adolescent Care organizations. He currently serves as the immediate past president of the board at Johnson Siding’s Volunteer Fire District.

“I’m ready to return to Pierre to continue my successful record representing SD’s families, protecting individual and private property rights, and sustaining the rational, effective, conservative policies that make South Dakota the best place in America to call home,” he said.

The Republican primary election is on June 2, with the general election on Nov. 3.

title: Dorothy Ann Amick (Flannery)
description: March 11, 1934 - March 17, 2026
_full_text:
March 11, 1934 - March 17, 2026

RAPID CITY - Dorothy Ann Amick (Flannery), 92, passed away Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at Peaceful Pines Senior Living Facility, Rapid City, South Dakota.

Dorothy was born March 11, 1934 in San Diego, California, the eldest child of John Clifford and Lois Estella (Cederlof) Flannery. As a child, she lived on various Naval bases in Florida, Hawaii and California. After her father retired from the Navy, they first lived in Placerville and then in Grizzly Flats, CA.

Dorothy graduated in 1952 from El Dorado County High School in Placerville, CA. She joined the U.S. Navy after graduation and was trained at Camp Lejeune, North Carolina as a Hospital Corpsman. Dorothy traveled the world as a Hospital Corpsman – Germany, Greece and Turkey are just a few of the countries she visited.

In 1962, she was stationed at Great Lakes Naval Base, Illinois where she met her future husband, Ronald C. Amick. They married July 27, 1963 and to this union were born four children. They settled in Rapid City after Ron separated from the Navy in 1969. Ron died in 2021 after 57 years of marriage.

Dorothy was preceded in death by her husband; parents; siblings, Ruthlee Hoehn and Clifford Flannery and niece, Julie Flannery.

Survivors include sons, Michael (Carol) Amick of Lead, and Patrick (Stacey) Amick of Flower Mound, Texas, and daughters, Celia (Lowell) Carlson of Rapid City, and Elizabeth (Steve) Maxwell of Blanco, Texs. Grandchildren, Anja Carlson of Rapid City, Joel (Aspin) Amick of Salt Lake City, Utah, Sean Amick of Orlando, FL, Leslie Amick and partner Matt Sell of Rogers, AR, and soon-to-be great-grandson, Carter Joseph Sell. Sister-in-law, Janet Flannery of California; nieces, Laura Flannery, Teresa and Tamra Simmons; grand-nieces, Candace, Jessica and Blair; and grand-nephew, Jacob.

Visitation will be from 4:30 pm to 6:30 pm with Vigil Service at 6:30 pm, Monday, March 23rd at Osheim & Schmidt Funeral Home, 2700 Jackson Blvd., Rapid City, SD.

Funeral services will be at 10 am, Tuesday, March 24th, at Blessed Sacrament Catholic Church, 4500 Jackson Blvd., Rapid City, SD. Burial follows at 12:30 pm at Black Hills National Cemetery, 20901 Pleasant Valley Drive, Sturgis, SD.

In lieu of flowers please make a donation to Dementia or Alzheimer's research:

Dementia Society of America - www.dementiasociety.org

Alzheimer's Association – www.alz.org
